issues.description: Energy-flows-one-way / matter-cycles distinction is correct, including that energy is ultimately lost as heat and is never recycled.

Direction of the 10% rule is correct: ~10% of energy is incorporated into the next level's biomass, ~90% lost to respiration/heat, egestion (feces), and uneaten parts. The 4–5 link consequence is standard.
issues.suggested_fix: No change. (Optional precision note: ~10% is an average/teaching convention; real ecological efficiencies range roughly 5–20%. Acceptable for AP as stated.)

issues.description: Energy budget 10,000 → 1,000 → 100 → 10 kcal applies x10% per step correctly.
Pause math is correct: 50,000 → 5,000 → 500 kcal for secondary consumers (third trophic level). The 'two steps up = x0.01' note is right.
NPP = GPP − producer respiration, and NPP (not GPP) is the energy available to consumers — stated correctly. Biome NPP ranking (rainforest/reef/estuary high; desert/deep ocean low) is accurate.
GPP/NPP trap framing is correct: GPP is the larger gross total, NPP is the remainder stored as new biomass and passed to the next level.

Core claims are correct: pyramid of energy is always upright; biomass and numbers pyramids can invert; phytoplankton supporting greater zooplankton standing biomass is the standard inverted-biomass example, and the 'at any moment / at any instant' standing-stock phrasing is the precise (and correct) way to state it. The only imprecision: labeling the inverted phytoplankton case as occurring because they are 'small, fast-reproducing' is fine, but the underlying cause is high turnover/short generation time (high productivity per unit biomass), not merely small size. Worth tightening so students attribute the inversion to turnover rate, not size alone.
issues.suggested_fix: Reword to: 'high-turnover, fast-reproducing phytoplankton are eaten so quickly that their standing biomass at any instant is smaller than the zooplankton they support.' Keep the 'at any moment' qualifier.

issues.description: Carbon reservoirs and fluxes are correct: photosynthesis fixes CO2, respiration releases it; long-term storage in fossil fuels, carbonate rock (limestone), and dissolved ocean CO2 is right. CO2 numbers ~280 ppm pre-industrial → >420 ppm (2024) are accurate (2024 annual mean ~422–424 ppm). Ocean acidification chemistry CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 → lower pH is correct.
issues.suggested_fix: No change. (Optional: H2CO3 dissociates to HCO3- + H+, and the released H+ is what lowers pH — accurate to add but not required.)

issues.description: All five steps and their microbes are correct: fixation N2 → NH3/NH4+ (Rhizobium, cyanobacteria, Haber-Bosch); nitrification NH4+ → NO2- → NO3- (Nitrosomonas then Nitrobacter, in that order); assimilation; ammonification (organic N → NH4+); denitrification NO3- → N2 by anaerobic bacteria. N2 = 78% of atmosphere and inert is correct.
Definition of fixation (atmospheric N2 → NH3/NH4+ only) and the correct exclusion of nitrification, assimilation, ammonification, and denitrification are all accurate.

overall_assessment: Scientifically sound throughout. The high-risk content the AP exam targets — direction of the 10% rule, NPP = GPP − producer respiration, energy pyramid always upright while biomass/numbers can invert, the five nitrogen steps with correct microbes (Nitrosomonas before Nitrobacter; denitrification anaerobic), the ~280→>420 ppm CO2 trajectory, and the energy-flows/matter-cycles distinction — is all correct, with the inverted-biomass example correctly hedged as a standing-stock ('at any instant') claim. No genuinely false statements that would teach a misconception. The single minor item is wording in section 12: attribute the inverted phytoplankton biomass to high turnover/short generation time rather than to small size alone. Verdict: minor.
